dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 respond. Patient alertness issue at an unknown residence in Westover Hills.
Sir, I am Daniel, a paramedic with the rescue squad. My partner is Maria. What happened today?
Um, our dad hasn't spoken much the last hour. His eyes seem locked right.
Mr. Thomas, please tell me your name and where we are now.
His eyes are open but turned fully right, not central.
Mr. Thomas, can you tell me what day it is?
Huh.
Mr. Thomas, can you squeeze my finger please?
Do you have trouble breathing or need anything? Sir?
Respirations are 16, steady, no distress noted.
No labored breathing. Chest is rising and falling evenly and moderately well.
Breathing fully appears unlabored at present time.
We'll be taking pulse and blood pressure now, sir. All normal signs will be noted.
Pulse is 104 beats-considered a tachycardia by normal ranges.
Blood pressure is 169 over 97, elevated systolic only slightly.
Oxygen saturation is 99 percent, indicating normal levels at this time.
Skin is warm, dry, and without cyanosis in general appearance.
Any recent changes in behavior or speech to your best knowledge?
He had rehab after a year-old stroke. For one week he was okay, but two days ago, he stopped talking and moving much more.
Has he been drinking or eating recently? Or experiencing pain anywhere in the body?
No, he has barely eaten or used the bathroom. Not in pain just not like himself at all since yesterday night perhaps.
On a 0 to 10 scale, where is his pain right now? 0 being no pain at all, 10 being worst possible pain he's had in life.
He doesn't seem in pain, so maybe a 0 rating but I can't be 100% sure because he won't talk much, even when asked again.
With your permission, I would like to briefly examine his eyes and pupils now.
Pupils are equal and small in size-about three millimeters right and left.
Please allow me to assess his chest for any possible breathing abnormalities or heart event response.
Lungs are clear. No wheezes, crackles, or effusions noted on auscultation at this point in time.
His face appears symmetric with no noted droop or visible hemiatrophy. Gums and tongue are moist within context of current minimal or provocation response at time of review.
Blood sugar is at 132, which is within a healthy range for his age and incidence gaps of activity.
You may detect a slight chill. This is the prep for four-lead monitoring attached to skin for EKG reading during baseline pretransport phase.
Four-lead EKG band is placed. Please allow us to run a cardiac reactivity chart now for baseline pretransport phase.
We are now placing the 12-lead EKG sensors to better assess heart response, note fair or possible arrhythmia at time of analysis for now as well.
12-lead EKG has returned with results-currently assessing for complete infarction undetermined age but sinus rhythm with possible PVCs main finding on lumen including Q-lead for II/aVF.
Utilizing protocol for altered mental status in male of this age data now for possible neurological evaluation at hospital in pretransport phase to 23.
Preparing now to remove and replace fluids in unit-IV line to start once in transit-blood pressure still within AFB at this point with next evaluation for more detail on dep.
He wanted to be sent to VCU if that is still your agreement for transport along with family regarding destination, by statement and family partnership?

Yes, that's where he intended as well for transport. Family is with him throughout procedures and also en route to facility with parking assistance planned ahead for arrival when possible to expedite process if needed.

Patient is now securely placed onto stretcher with three-strap harnesses or full width for safety during transport through curvatures in access to facility, evident minimal but easily navigational time once within 23 community per prior approach.

Transport in progress with no anomalies in vitals yet. We are monitoring heart and breath closely during transit phase albeit time advances due to power transition from 0 NEMO unit for driving module.
No changes detected yet in consciousness or baseline vital signs with slight improvement to decrease in pulse from 104 down to 90, stable respiration at 16 in two hour interval now.

Medical record number (MRN) was successful noted, thanks to family documents and reports used for standard PICC access by physician or nurse as well to expedite care once in front of requesting facility from VCU.
Patient was unable sign during pretransport phase due to his current altered status and unresponsiveness to communicative stimuli in stated orientation phase at time of check in with planner.

Eye movement is fixed to one side, but with some minimal voluntary eye tracking observed in right side, indicative toward possible motor commands or sensory response from left portions in right temporal area.
Breathing remains steady at 16 with no primary need for oxygen support given oxygen saturation measurement at 99% on pretransport phase baseline vitals.

New pulse is lower at current time, still in cyanotic free zone and adequately warm per patient skin assessment, indicating better blood flow but still must monitor closely for sudden drop or other cardiovascular incident.

Current pulse sits at 90 beats per minute-slightly lower from 104 to 90 in current span of time. In stable condition but tachycardia still flagged at baseline within initial transport phase back to VCU.

Current BP reports out 165 over 98-elevated systolic with minimal gain but no signs of hemodynamic change from prior recording. Stable range at this time with no overdose-type signs or known toxicity reversal necessary at present.

EKG shows now as sinus rhythm overall once adjustments are made for baseline PVCs-as in, no significant new interval creation for further dilated response mono or lateral trace as well as standard sinus pathway chemically intervention regarding infarct quantity reconditions evaluation.

Mild JVD observed in assessment phase but with no lower lobe crackles or with effusion signs of left ventricular dysfunctions alongside. Initial interpretation shows no immediate action for reverting but considered as moderate degree concern according to probable calculated values from patient initial age range 81.

His records show past stroke, Alzheimer's, and hypertension. Any other conditions or events? Mentioning by his medical history as in age, concern deeper here affecting his neurological response potentials at emergency site per individual patient data.

Correct-stroke, Alzheimers, and hypertension with no other current pending medical history or new conditions that are actionable yet or worsening supposedly once event hits the patient suddenly within his treatment phase from family perspective in these few days to capture the issue's detail in time.
Patient has a known medication-metoprolol succinate-and has no tracker gel patches or temporary over-the-counter procuring either.
We have checked and no allergies are on file for patient prescribed medication or non-prescribed as well.
He takes metoprolol-no other medications adding on temporarily, including non-steroidal, sleeping aid, or diuretic compounds, known or unavailable by family for transfer.

Respirations stayed at 16, overall stable with no increase or decrease beyond 2 per minute current span of approximately an hour. By visual and audible analysis seems slow but not elevated for spontaneous breathing.

Oxygen saturation remains stable with 99% again, also rechecked and valid for baseline data at current attached ambient without added oxygen intake at process stages mentioned before or now during baseline or transport.

The use of oxygen is not necessary at this time. It is noted your saturation readings remain above 95% under non-supported airway conditions with maintained patent airway as stated-no oxygen therapy was initiated at any stage in transport process for this excision.

When last did he have a meal or drink anything at all-food or fluids in last several hours or days specifically to capture his degenerating condition at this audit stage from family grasp to detect initial signs earlier during visit with his family hours in care as sender to this spot pretransport.

Last intake was about 72 hours ago, with him eating marginal amounts, even given enticements and family-supervised, watching him for any improvement-no clicks of recovery backwards but lots of decreasing range in intake considering hospital strains over the years in his condition.
What led to this event? Was there an incident, something that immediately changed his behavior at or before time of 911 call?

He was just resting in the chair-started without apparent trigger or immediate change of condition, apart from ongoing progressive decline in abilities and consciousness as reflected in prior days now leading to call being made at time of lack of response one more time from family side for medical intervention considering stroke or secondary attack per old episode from time of rechecking and family trust in demise from this view tonight for transmission of trauma by alarm device call.

Mr. Thomas, could you try and lift left hand, as assessor typically asks for limb comparison to evaluate possible unilateral inactivity without resistance due to mental issue from potential stroke or intracranial internal passage improper effect or restricted or related reaction secondary to prior established CVA procedure you are expected to face in tag conjunction medical note.

Hmm-will attempt lift but result in minimal effort or CSR response from AP designation and subsequent automated response that may be absent or mildly voluntary under these neurological as well as behavioral assumptions of inherited mental degeneration for severe consequence if left unmet as suggested in normative parameters for administering acute response at this event---

Any pain in chest when palpation occurs or discomfort anywhere-remains a zero in perception in interaction modes as well as baseline while spontaneous reaction or sudden incidence due to cardiac imbalance or miss-functioning heart event here via recorded reactivity?

Not responded in standard refusal or presence but unable to assess because of mental status decline. No verbal or arm signal was given to confirm pain or discomfort in central chest or left side for now confirmed by intuitive survey and relied-only on assessment for possible non-painful chest anatomical features during operation for examination at point of progress to hospital next.

Any pain or unusual sensations in extremities or back, or leg, arm areas-since patient shows no spontaneous limb use or unilateral preference from grocery database indicators introduced at time of consumption or parallel data collection in lifestyle choices?

His limbs appear okay to shape but he's not moving them much. His arms and legs are also not showing any deformities or abnormal angulations for impactful transportation reasons at that point and for health effects observed during observation window from home as per current condition.

Pain is still assessed as 0-no change in status or newer perceptions from him or visual inspection due to equivalent disease or stroke residual behind analysis and sensory observation guide at time of original call or transport critical phase notation.

GCS remains 11-E4, V2, and M5 as previously noted in physical and non-verbal interaction factor per se should reflect adequacy of grading and remains stable for duration of contact and movement before hospital for ER implementation purpose.

Based on age and condition at current time, he should undergo neurological evaluation again at hospital for potential thrombus or aneurysm or spontaneous vasculature issue once he arrives-given the history of stroke, and caution is needed in diagnostics for reevent with CVA diagnosis as flagged in narrative document for whole day long.

We have given all chief contention, present condition, and prior medical information for his hospitalization and care by collective team to emergency ER staff on the other side now at post VCU to be assessed for further action.D

Patient was transferred onto hospital ER bed from stretcher with no discomfort or change once notes were clearly passed off. All vital signs and EC notes confirmed at hospital's admission ward as proper protocol phase for receiving and moving throughout emergency room wards or beyond.
All steps have been done successfully under altered mental status protocol. From identified CVA flagged always as possible in this case of note.

A 20-gauge IV was placed in the accessory cephalic vein with normal saline running as sort of provision done now in transport-no infusion needed as yet for immediate emergency or blood pooling or distribution through this access target for possible medication should arise reduce in critical moments as targeted intervention.

Full medical history paper and EKG 12-lead findings are filed with attending physician, ready to be viewed or assessed during ER RN or doctor's detailed rounds or as ordered or from physician communication lines in hospital pathways.

For the ER nurse on duty, his history includes stroke, hypertension, and Alzheimer's. All flags were raised in report sent, no further contradictory notes at time of this step in rescue assessment and handling.
IV in place over transit-no infusions being administered as the condition remains stable with low acuity during transition for now conclude for all actions pending.

Emergency bed is ready-rolling him now into ER after completing dual harness securing on stretcher without incident during maneuvering hospital premises doorway restrictions synchronized between hew vehicles and admissions team on mobile routing phase.

Eyes remain fixed, though some minor movement and tracking was observable during pre-visual evaluation toward point of transition and this is reported in field sheet for their review after completion of longitudinal IM documentation and evaluation as well.
